sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
अयतिः श्रद्धयोपेतो योगाच्चलितमानसः ।
अप्राप्य योगसंसिद्धिं कां गतिं कृष्ण गच्छति ॥ ३७ ॥

anvaya.meaning: the unrestrained one / one without sustained effort

translations.verbatim_quote: Arjuna said: O Kṛṣṇa, what is the destination of the unsuccessful transcendentalist, who in the beginning takes to the process of self-realization with faith but who later desists due to worldly-mindedness and thus does not attain perfection in mysticism?

Arjuna said: O Krishna, what is the destination of the unsuccessful transcendentalist, who in the beginning takes to the process of self-realization with faith but who later desists due to worldly-mindedness and thus does not attain perfection in mysticism?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2074 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The path of self-realization or mysticism is described in the Bhagavad-gītā. The basic principle of self-realization is knowledge that the living entity is not this material body but that he is different from it and that his happiness is in eternal life, bliss and knowledge.

Does he not perish like a small piece of cloud torn from a large mass of cloud — pe

positions.rendering: the unsuccessful transcendentalist endowed with faith but failing to attain perfection
endowed with faith in yoga but not making the effort of yoga (an-ayati)
faith-endowed but lacking in sustained effort, fallen from yoga before completing it

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings agree on the essential structure: faith was present, but sustained effort failed. The category named is the genuinely-attempting practitioner who fell short — not one who never tried. This is the precondition for the consolation in 6.40-6.45.

literal_meaning: The speaker said: the one endowed with faith but unrestrained, his mind fallen away from yoga, not attaining the perfection of yoga — what destination does he reach, O Krishna?
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.37 opens the fallen-yogi question. The compound is precise: ayatiḥ (without sustained restraint, having failed at saṁyama) BUT śraddhayopeta (endowed with faith) — the practitioner who has had genuine faith but has not been able to maintain the practice; calita-mānasaḥ (whose mind has wandered from yoga); aprāpya yoga-saṁsiddhim (without having attained the perfection of yoga). What gati (state / destination) does such a one reach? Sankara's reading: he was endowed with faith in yoga but he was an ayati — not making the effort of yoga; his mind has slipped from yoga; what is his fate? Ramanuja's reading: one who is faith-endowed but lacking in sustained effort, fallen from yoga before completing it — what becomes of him? Both traditions emphasise that the question is about the genuinely-attempting practitioner who falls short. The verse is the operational analog of every senior who has had a real practice and lost it.
